observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the provision of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992, which requires that only idle funds in the General Fund can be deposited under time deposit accounts upon prior authority of the Sanggunian and the approval of the Local Chief Executive; and
Stop the practice of investing Special Education Fund and Trust Fund under time deposit accounts as these funds have specific purposes and do not fall within the context of idle fund pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-382.

We recommended that Management require:
The Division/City/District Superintendent to prepare budget for the school board supported by specific PPAs for the ensuing fiscal year as required in Sections 100(b) and 272 of RA No. 7160;
The concerned City Officials to validate the SEF budget for consistency with the AIP and APP as required in Article 410 of the IRR of RA No.7160; and
The concerned City Officials strictly adhere to the provision of Section 7.2 of the IRR of RA No.9184 mandating that all government procurement shall be in accordance with the APP, consistent with its duly approved budget.

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee make reasonable projections of collectible revenues, particularly real property tax, which is the basis of the annual budget for the SEF, in accordance with Section 235 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Management:
Create the DRRMO which is mandated by law to be responsible for the development of plans and programs, oversee the implementation and undertake coordinated efforts on disaster risk management programs and activities of the city;
Maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF from current year’s appropriation and prior years’ unexpended balance so that programs, projects and activities related to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness program will be implemented within the scheduled timeframes. In such manner, the in-house capability to efficiently manage all types of emergencies and to achieve orderly transitions from response to sustained recovery in case of disaster/calamity will be strengthened to improve the disaster resilience of communities in the City; and
Advise the concerned officials of the City to prepare the LDRMMIF indicating the list of priority projects and activities for the unexpended balance of LDRRMF for prior years. In such manner, the optimum amount of LDRRMF will be used efficiently and effectively for the intended purpose.

We recommended that:
The concerned officials undertake appropriate actions to secure relevant documents to warrant the validity of recorded receivables amounting to P132,887,018.49 as well as to recover the amount due the City Government including penalty thereon, if any; and
The City Accountant effect necessary adjustments to correct the recording of dishonored checks pertaining to payment of Real Property Taxes under the appropriate accounts, RPT Receivable and Deferred RPT Income; and to reduce the balance of Cash in Bank and Government Equity accounts by P2,339,452.50for the unearned income.

We recommended that the Management:
Ensure that the utilization of the 20% Development Fund partakes the nature of investment or capital expenditures and the cost of recovery and entrepreneurship should be taken into consideration;
Implement immediately the identified programs and projects as embodied in the AIP; and
Prioritize projects programmed under the 20% development fund that yield greater benefit to the people in accordance with DBM-DILG Joint Circular 2011-1.

We recommended that the Management consider the codification of the revenue rules and regulations of the City to provide a clearer reading and ready reference to taxpayers and implementers. We further recommended that Management update the existing tax rates to be able to sustain an adequate, appropriate and relevant tax rates in relation to the City’s fast-paced growth and development requirements.

We recommended that:
The Management create IAS unit, under the administrative supervision and control of the head of the agency to institute and conduct a program of internal audit for the agency, in compliance with Budget Circular No. 2004-4 to ensure that the government spends within means, invests in the right priorities, and delivers measurable results for greater transparency and accountability;
The concerned City officials submit a proposal to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the establishment of the Internal Audit Service/Unit in the City, including the organizational structure, functional charts, personnel complement, operating budget and funding source; and
The Management adopt the Internal Audit Manual for Local Government Units as a guide in establishing, maintaining and operating an Internal Audit Service/Unit (IAS/U), pursuant to DBM Circular No. 110 dated June 10, 2016.

We recommended that:
The City Treasurer give priority to the registration of the vehicles pursuant to Section 5 (a) of RA No. 4136;
The CGSO ensure that all city-owned motor vehicles are registered promptly with the LTO as government vehicles under the name of the City (either with red plate or security plate), obtain all required documents to facilitate registration of  vehicles and maintain complete records of registration with the LTO, as basis for the subsequent renewal of registration; and
The concerned officials determine the persons responsible for the late renewal of the registration so that refund of the penalties can be sought from them, otherwise, deduct the amount of surcharges and penalties from the salaries or whatever benefits due the responsible employees.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant ensure that DVs for Monetization are duly supported with approved Application for Leave specifying the request for monetization, with sufficient leave credit balance to cover the number of days applied for, as certified by the Human Resource Management Office; and
The Human Resource Management Officer properly monitor and control the granting or payment of monetization of leave credits in accordance with the provisions of CSC MC No. 41 s. of 1998.

We recommended that:
The concerned Officials see to it that contracts issued to all consultants are in accordance with the pro-forma contract of service containing the necessary stipulations as required under CSC Memorandum No. 24, s. 2002, as amended. This would ensure that the hiring of consultants is properly justified and actually needed by the City. The contracts should also be signed by the hired consultants and duly notarized; and
The Accountant submit all the required documentation before processing and payment of the claims for salaries of the consultants.

We recommended that:
All Department Heads conduct adequate planning of the work activities of their staff and to see to it that the individual work assignments, as stated in their request for authority to render overtime services, are thoroughly reviewed.  Strictly follow the Guidelines on the Authority and Compensation of Overtime Services before recommending the same for approval of the Local Chief Executive to avoid the unnecessary cash payment of overtime work and the following should be considered:
Name and position of employees allowed to render overtime
Specific duties to be undertaken for which overtime is allowed
Maximum number of overtime hours allowed per employee
Officials who shall supervise the overtime;
The City Accountant review thoroughly the overtime claims submitted for payment whether the requirements for payment of overtime were met and that rules and regulations were strictly followed or applied in accordance with Section 5.10 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that:
The concerned City Officials comply with its obligation pursuant to RMO No. 23-2014 as withholding agents for purposes of the creditable tax required to be withheld; and
The City Accountant compute the taxes to be withheld based on the tax rates provided by RA 8424 otherwise known as the National Internal Revenue Code and remit the same to the BIR.

We recommended that:
The concerned personnel submit the required documents under Budget Circular No. 2007-2 dated October 1, 2007 to facilitate the verification of the validity and propriety of the payments of honoraria; and
The concerned officials stop the payment of honoraria to officers and members of the committees performing functions inherent to their regular duties and responsibilities.  The officers and members of said committees should be required to refund the honoraria paid to them.

We recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies provided under the laws, rules and regulations in enforcing immediate compliance of the audit suspensions and the refund/settlement of the disallowances as prescribed by Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.
